title: Clank & Slop — The Tape
tagline: — READ THE TAPE · ALL THE SLOP THAT'S FIT TO PRICE —

props.label: At a Glance · From the Tape Desk

bullets.text: The Strait moved from freeze to probation. Kpler tracked four Qatar-controlled LNG tankers heading into Hormuz on Monday and three VLCCs exiting Sunday, while CENTCOM counted 55 merchant ships carrying 17M+ barrels on Saturday; Sunday traffic fell to five vessels against 26, so one strong day and one weak day do not equal a reopened chokepoint against the ~120/day pre-war norm.

Brent followed the bridge, not the rhetoric — down 2.09% to $78.89 by 0633 GMT after touching $82.30, tying the fade to the Lake Lucerne readout and Iran's claimed oil and petrochemical waivers. The stress migrated into freight and insurance: LNG freight near $100,000/day, a Chubb-led $400M war-risk consortium, and Aramco cutting July OSPs to Asia by $6/bbl.

Sunday night's blast at Ras Laffan's Barzan facility left 54 injured and 18 missing, but the asset is a domestic-gas project — pipeline gas for local industry, power and desalination — not one of the 14 LNG export trains. Ras Laffan moves about 20% of global LNG, so location gives the incident relevance, but the categories stay separate without evidence of sabotage.

The fee story stays narrower than the rumor: Reuters reported claims of at least one $2M payment but could not confirm them, while Iran's PGSA permit regime offers free coverage during a 60-day window and reserves the right to introduce future fees. Tasnim kept the hook alive by tying Hormuz reopening to a Lebanon ceasefire — passage linked back to compliance.

props.title: Hormuz: one strong day, one weak day
props.subtitle: Strait transit reality · vessels
props.source: CENTCOM (55 merchant ships, Saturday), Reuters / Kpler (5 vessels Sunday vs 26 Saturday), Reuters (~120/day pre-conflict commercial norm)

items.what: Hormuz: insured tonnage return + the fee question
items.why: Normality requires sustained two-way transits, falling war-risk premia, no enforced fee, and a multi-day return of liftings — not just unattributable hulls slipping through; the unconfirmed $2M-payment claim and the PGSA 60-day free window are the things to settle.

items.what: Lake Lucerne implementation · the Lebanon de-confliction cell
items.why: The first round produced a High Level Committee and a 60-day roadmap but not an enforceable sequence; Araghchi called the Lebanon mechanism the first real test, yet neither Israel nor Hezbollah signed, so the cell is where committees either turn into compliance or fail visibly.

The Security Council has demanded the RSF halt an imminent offensive on a city the HRC says puts ~500,000 civilians at risk; ten days of drone strikes and encirclement are running without verified ground entry, so the prevention window is open precisely because the full assault is not yet confirmed.

The Air Force split production airframes (FQ-42, FQ-44) from a six-vendor mission-autonomy pool; the durable contest moves toward reference architectures, test loops and replaceable behavior software as the autonomy base leaves the demonstrator phase.

Two routes around the access chokepoint surfaced within ten days: Sakana's Fugu orchestration sells continuity rather than provenance, while Qwable's disputed traces turn restricted outputs into reusable training material — access controls slow direct use but do not cleanly stop approximation or leakage.

items.what: Lloyd's List reported crude tanker freight facing a major repricing if the Hormuz reopening holds, while LNG freight is likely to stay near $100,000 a day until owners and insurers trust the truce; war-risk structures are expanding, including a Chubb-led $400M consortium — a hull cleared by Iran can still be commercially blocked by insurance and finance.

Dubai, Oman and Murban differentials moved into discount as stranded barrels prepare to re-enter the market and Saudi Aramco cut July OSPs to Asia by $6 a barrel, though those prices stayed above pre-war levels — the discount is the market clearing fear, inventory and returning supply at once.

Monday's shipping signal — four Qatar-controlled LNG tankers heading into Hormuz despite reduced traffic — argues against an immediate visible halt in Qatar's LNG movement, without proving Barzan escaped damage; March attacks had already removed 12.8 mtpa, or 17% of export capacity, for three to five years.
